8tate of Califor1l..ia 
Fair Political Practices Commissioll 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Mr. Br ian Car r 
Administration 
322·5660 
Office of the County Counsel 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
March 15, 1983 
County Government Center, East Wing 
70 West Hedding Street 
San Jose, CA 
95110 
Enforcement 
322-6«1 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Our Advice No. A-83-046 
Dear Mr. Carr: 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on 
Dehalf of County Supervisor Thomas Legan. 
Mr. Legan has asked 
wnether he can vote on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for 
Santa Clara County. 
According to your letter, Mr. Legan is employed by Kaiser 
Cement, which is a source of income to him within the meaning of 
Government Code Section 87103.11 
Kaiser Cement is among the 
half-dozen largest Dusinesses in the unincorporated area of 
Santa Clara County. 
The proposed ordinance would have a 
material financial effect on Kaiser Cement, within the meaning 
of Government Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18702. 
The ordinance would also affect a "great many" other 
businesses in the unincorporated area, although the number of 
Dusinesses which would be affected, and the potential extent of 
the effect on eaCh busines, is unKnown at this time. 
CONCLUSION 
It is our advlce, based upon the facts you have provided, 
that Mr. Legan should disqualify himself from voting on, or 
participatlng in consideration of, the ordinance. 
11 
All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise seated. 


Mr. Brian Carr 
Ma r ch 15, 198 3 
Page 2 
ANALYSIS 
As a public official Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from 
participation in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeaole that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguish-
able from its effect on the pUblic generally, upon a source of 
income to him as defined in Section 87103. 
Since you have 
stated that Kaiser Cement is a source of income to Mr. Legan, 
and that the proposed ordinance would have a material financial 
effect on Kaiser, Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from all 
consideration of the ordinance unless the effect of the ordinance 
on Kaiser would not be distinguishable from its effect on the 
pUblic generally. 
Section 87103. 
For the "puolic generally" exception to apply to this 
situation, the ordinance would have to affect a "significant 
segment" of the puolic, and it would have to affect Kaiser 
Cement "in substantially the same manner" as it would affect the 
other memoers of that segment of tne pUblic. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code 
Section 18703. 
On the basis of the facts you have provided, I 
cannot say that either aspect of this test is met. 
It is undouotedly true that a great many ousinesses will be 
affected by the ordinance; however, "a great many" businesses do . 
not necessarily constitute a significant segment of the pUblic.ll 
There will certainly oe a large numoer of bUSinesses which will 
not be affected at all by the orainance. 
Although there are no 
firm guidelines for determining what constitutes a significant 
segment of the public, the Commission has considered the public 
generally issue on several occasions. 
It has held that 50% of 
all commercial property owners do not constitute a significant 
segment of the PUblic generally. 
Brown Opinion, 4 FPPC 19, .23 
(1978). 
Owners of four or more units of rental housing do not 
constitute a significant segment of the puolic. 
Ferraro 
Opinion, 4 FPPC 62 (1978). 
On the other hand, all retail 
business establishments in a core area of the City of Davis 
were held to constitute a significant segment of the pUblic 
generally. 
Owen Opinion, 2 FPPC 77 (1976). 
Based on these 
decisions, and absent more complete facts, it appears most 
likely that the number of businesses which will be affected by 
the ordinance is not sufficient to constitute a significant 
segment of the public. 
l;
1 
You have stated that Kaiser is not part of the 
"predominan t indus try" in the county. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Sec tion 
l8703(c) . 


Mr. Brian Carr 
March 15, 1983 
Page 3 
There is also some doubt as to whether the second half of 
the pUblic generally test is met. 
Even if enough businesses to 
constitute a significant segment of the puolic generally were 
affected by the ordinance, Mr. Legan would have to disqualify 
himself unless Kaiser Cement were affected "in substantially the 
same manner" as the rest of .the businesses affected. 
Again, 
without more facts, we cannot be certain how the effect on 
Kaiser would compare with the effects on other businesses. 
However, given the fact that Kaiser is one of the half-dozen 
largest businesses in the area, and the fact that the ordinance 
imposes different types of requirements on different types of 
hazardous wastes, it seems most likely that the effect on Kaiser 
will differ substantially, both in kind and in magnitude, from 
the effect on other businesses. 
The facts as you have stated them are clearly not sufficient 
to establish that the pUblic generally exception applies to 
Mr. Legan's situation. 
On the contrary, on the basis of the 
given facts, it seems most liKely that the effect of the pro-
posed ordinance on Kaiser Cement will be distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. 
Therefore our advice is 
that Mr. Legan should disqualify himself from participation in 
consideration of the proposed ordinance. 
Jf course, it is 
possiole that, given more complete facts, our advice could 
change. 
We would be happy to reconSlder your question if you 
want to make a fuller factual presentation. 
BAl.'1: km 
Very truly your?, 
,rf{Ltk~ Yh&uUlu-
/
Barbara A. Milman 
Chief, Legal Division 


County of Santa Clara 
California 
Ms. Barbara Milman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
Legal Division 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95804 
Dear Ms. Mi Iman: 
Office of the County Counsel 
County Government Center, East Wing 
70 West Hedding Street 
San Jose, California 95110 
299-2111 
Area Code 408 
Donald L Clark, County Counsel 
Pursuant to our telephone conversation last week, 
we are 
herein 
sting written advice 
from 
the 
Fair 
Political 
Practices 
Commission 
as 
provided 
by 
Government 
Code 
§ 
831l4(b). 
Our question is whether County Supervisor Thomas 
Legan, 
who is employed by Ka iser Cement I 
can part icipa te in 
the deci sion maki ng process invol vi ng 
a 
proposed hazardous 
materials ordinance. 
For the purposes of 
s letter, we ask you to assume that 
the ordinance would have a material effect on Kaiser, and that 
Kaiser is a "financial interest" as defined by Government Code 
§ 87103. 
Therefore, we believe the key question is whether 
the effect on Kaiser is "distinguishable from its effect on 
the public 
ly." 
(§ 87103) 
In re spondi ng to thi s quest ion, we under stand you may be 
guided by 2 Cal. Admin. Code § 18703 which defines the phrase 
"effect on the public generally." 
Under 
that 
regulation, 
where 
the financial interest is included within the 
"pre-
dominant" industry in the jurisdiction, it would be considered 
a 
"si gni f icant segment" of the publ ic and there would be no 
prohibition on participation in the decision making process. 
Al though cement production is not the predominant industry in 
thi s 
juri sdi ction, the hazardous waste ord 
e 
would apply 
to all industry in the jurisdiction. 
The effect of the or-
dinance is therefore even more general than if it 
ied only 
to a 
inant" industry. 
Our understandi 
is that besides Ka 
hal 
n 1 
st busine s 
in the 
bus 
n the 
the 
More 
i 
ser 
othe 
ine se 
t 
An Equal Opportunity Employer 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
-2-
February 16, 1983 
We believe your answer to this question may determine the 
degree to which individuals in the business community can take 
part in governmental decisions affecting the general 
com-
muni 
We 
there 
awai t 
your response 
th considerable 
i nt erest. 
Should you need further i 
ormation. please feel free to 
contact me. 
:mw 
cc: 
vi sor Thomas 
Very truly yours, 
DONALD L. CLARK 
County Counsel

sections.question_synthetic: Can County Supervisor Thomas Legan participate in a decision on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance when he is employed by Kaiser Cement, a company that would be materially affected by the ordinance?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Mr. Legan must disqualify himself from voting on and participating in consideration of the hazardous waste ordinance because the ordinance would have a material financial effect on Kaiser Cement that is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request (February 16, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (March 15, 1983). The Question section was synthesized from the stated facts and context in the FPPC's response letter. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not significantly affect meaning.

QUESTION: Mr. Legan has asked whether he can vote on a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for Santa Clara County. According to your letter, Mr. Legan is employed by Kaiser Cement, which is a source of income to him within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103. Kaiser Cement is among the half-dozen largest businesses in the unincorporated area of Santa Clara County. The proposed ordinance would have a material financial effect on Kaiser Cement, within the meaning of Government Code Section 87103 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702.

CONCLUSION: It is our advlce, based upon the facts you have provided, 
that Mr. Legan should disqualify himself from voting on, or 
participatlng in consideration of, the ordinance. 
11 
All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code unless otherwise seated. 


Mr. Brian Carr 
Ma r ch 15, 198 3

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that County Supervisor Thomas Legan, who is employed by Kaiser Cement, must disqualify himself from voting on and participating in a proposed hazardous waste ordinance for Santa Clara Clara County because the ordinance would have a material financial effect on his employer that does not meet the 'public generally' exception.
